political.s: FEC data shows Target's PAC raised approximately $359K with roughly 54% directed to Republican candidates and 46% to Democrats, reflecting a broadly bipartisan posture. OpenSecrets reports total 2024 cycle contributions of ~$1.36M and federal lobbying spend of $2.76M. Target also donated $1M to the Trump-Vance Inaugural Committee in late 2024, the first such inauguration donation in over a decade, signaling a pragmatic rightward pivot.

charity.s: In 2024, Target and the Target Foundation donated $406 million in total, with $396 million directed to organizations with local community impact. Target team members volunteered over 1 million hours in 2024, the ninth time in company history reaching that milestone. Charitable focus areas include education, food security, and community equity programs.
charity.focus: Community impact, education, food security, racial equity (historically)

environment.s: Target achieved a 41.3% reduction in absolute emissions from operations compared to a 2017 baseline and sourced more than 75% of its operational electricity from renewable sources in 2024, surpassing its 2025 goal of 60%. The company diverted 87% of operational waste from landfills and has committed to net zero GHG emissions by 2040. Target has received EPA ENERGY STAR Partner of the Year for sustained excellence for nine consecutive years, with over 1,500 stores ENERGY STAR certified.

labor.s: Target has approximately 415,000 team members and has no collective bargaining agreements covering its active workforce. The company announced a starting wage range of $15–$24/hour and expanded health benefits to hourly workers averaging 25+ hours/week, along with tuition-free education benefits. However, the company has faced historical NLRB cases and past contractor labor violations, including documented wage theft by subcontractors, though Target did eventually adopt a Responsible Contractor Policy.

dei.s: Target rolled back its DEI programs in January 2025, ending three-year DEI hiring goals, withdrawing from the HRC Corporate Equality Index, and terminating its REACH racial equity initiative and minority supplier diversity program. This followed years of progressive DEI commitments spurred in part by the 2020 murder of George Floyd in Target's hometown of Minneapolis. The rollback triggered boycotts, investor lawsuits, and significant foot-traffic declines, placing Target in the 'mixed' category given its prior leadership and abrupt reversal.

animals.s: Target as a retailer sells both cruelty-free and non-cruelty-free brands, and its own house brand Up&Up has not publicly disclosed a full cruelty-free policy. On farm animal welfare, Target has made commitments to cage-free eggs (reaching 66% in 2024) but has failed to fulfill decade-old pledges to eliminate gestation crates for pork, drawing criticism from Humane World for Animals. Target does maintain animal welfare standards for food suppliers grounded in the Five Domains framework.

guns.s: Target does not sell firearms or ammunition in its stores and has not done so for many years, distinguishing it from competitors like Walmart. The company has no known firearms manufacturing operations. Target's policy has remained consistently gun-free across its nearly 2,000 U.S. store locations.

privacy.s: Target collects extensive customer data including behavioral, biometric (via in-store surveillance cameras), and transactional data, and shares it with affiliates, banking partners, and third-party vendors. A 2024 federal court denied Target's motion to dismiss a class-action lawsuit alleging illegal collection of biometric data without consent under Illinois BIPA. Target operates Roundel, an in-house retail media network that monetizes customer data for targeted advertising, raising additional privacy concerns.

execPay.s: CEO Brian Cornell received total compensation of $20.4 million in FY2024 (per SEC proxy), including $1.4M base salary and $16.1M in stock awards, up 6.25% year-over-year despite declining company performance. The AFL-CIO Paywatch confirms the $20.4M figure for fiscal year ending 2024. A historical peak CEO-to-median-worker ratio of approximately 680:1 was reported in prior years; the current ratio is lower due to reduced equity award vesting tied to performance shortfalls.
execPay.ratio: ~680:1 (historical peak); current ratio lower due to performance-based equity reductions
